title: 1  Introduction
text:
This is a book created from markdown and executable code.
See Knuth (1984) for additional discussion of literate programming.

1 + 1

[1] 2






Knuth, Donald E. 1984. “Literate Programming.” Comput. J. (USA) 27 (2): 97–111. https://doi.org/10.1093/comjnl/27.2.97.

title: 2  Quiz
text:
Choose the R function that makes each statement valid.

      

        
          Use 
          
            ---
            dbinom()
            pbinom()
            pnorm()
            prop.table(margin = 1)
            prop.table(margin = 2)
            qnorm()
          
          to quantify the area under the normal curve, to the left of a specific value.
        

        
          Obtain a table of probabilities, all of which are conditioned on the variable placed at the right of the plus sign in xtabs(), after piping it into 
          
            ---
            dbinom()
            pbinom()
            pnorm()
            prop.table()
            prop.table(margin = 1)
            prop.table(margin = 2)
          
          .
        

        
          The 
          
            ---
            dbinom()
            pbinom()
            prop.table()
            prop.table(margin = 1)
            prop.table(margin = 2)
            qnorm()
          
          function determines the total probability from zero up to your target value in a binomial distribution.
        

        
          To get a joint probability table, pipe the cross-tabulated data from xtabs() directly into
          
            ---
            pbinom()
            pnorm()
            prop.table()
            prop.table(margin = 1)
            prop.table(margin = 2)
            qnorm()
          
          .
        

      

    

    
    

      

      
        Airlines often overbook to hedge against no-shows. For example, if 140 tickets are sold for a 134-seat flight where passengers have an independent 9% chance of not showing up, what is the probability that the airline gets fewer than 6 no-shows?
      

      
         0.7%
         1.1%
         1.6%
         2.7%
         97%
         99%
      

    

    
    

      

      
        Suppose that events A and B each have nonzero probability. Determine whether each statement is TRUE or FALSE.
      

      

        
          
            ---
            TRUE
            FALSE
          
          When A and B are independent, it follows that P(A | not B) = P(not B).
        

        
          
            ---
            TRUE
            FALSE
          
          The addition rule simplifies to P(A and B) = P(A) + P(B) when A and B are mutually exclusive.
        

        
          
            ---
            TRUE
            FALSE
          
          The joint probability P(A and B) is equal to zero as long as A and B are independent.
        

        
          
            ---
            TRUE
            FALSE
          
          If A and B are complementary, then P(A) = 1 − P(B).
        

      

    

    
    

      

      
        As a major winter storm looms on the radar, a rookie meteorologist notes that there is exactly a 13% chance of snow.
        If they want the probability it will NOT snow, what rule must they apply?
      

      
         addition
         delulu
         multiplication
         negation
         rizz
         three-month
      

    

    
    

      

      
        In a controlled cognitive experiment, participants are asked to press a button as soon as a visual stimulus appears on a screen.
        Each participant completes many trials. Because reaction time is influenced by many small factors—attention, neural processing speed,
        fatigue, and momentary distractions—the recorded reaction times vary from trial to trial.

        Researchers find that reaction times (in milliseconds) for a typical adult under these conditions are approximately normal,
        with a mean of 250ms and standard deviation of 40ms.
      

      
        There is roughly a 19% chance that an adult has a reaction time longer than 215ms.
        The IQR of reaction times is close to 54ms.
        Given that an adult’s reaction time is shorter than 250ms, the probability it is longer than 210ms is approximately 84%
      

      
         1 only
         2 only
         3 only
         1 and 2 only
         1 and 3 only
         2 and 3 only
         All
         None
      

    

    
    

      

      
        Among the great selection of amusements and rides in a typical fair is a quick game of rolling a biased die.
        The distribution of the die’s faces is tabulated below.
      

      



face
1
2
3
4
5
6


prob
0.17
0.15
0.09
0.19
0.21
0.19





      
        You have to shell out $3 to play the game. The mechanics are simple:
      

      
        If you land on a face lower than 3, your payout is $12.
        If you land on a face higher than 3, you pay the game operator $4.
        Otherwise, your payout is $28.
      

      Which of the following statements is supported by the information provided?

      
         On average, playing this game many times leads to a small positive gain of about $1 per play.
         Since face 5 is the most likely outcome and has a positive payoff, the game must be profitable overall.
         The game is fair because the gains and losses are balanced across the different outcomes.
         Because more than half of the outcomes lead to a loss, the game must have a negative expected value.
         The expected value can be estimated by looking mainly at the most likely outcome (face 5), since it contributes most to the average.
         The average result over many plays will be close to zero, so the small gains and losses mostly cancel out.
      

    

    
    

      

      
        At the peak of operational pressure, the City of Austin Animal Shelter undertook a comprehensive review of its historical intake and outcome records. The goal was to better understand how early intake conditions might relate to eventual adoption outcomes.
      

      
        For each animal, two binary variables were recorded:
      

      
        stray: whether the animal was taken in as a stray (stray / not stray)
        adopted: whether the animal was ultimately adopted (adopted / not adopted)
      

      
        To explore the relationship between intake condition and outcome, the analyst computed:
      

      xtabs(~ stray + adopted, data = shelter) %>%
  prop.table(margin = 2) %>%
  round(2)
      
      With the output being:

                 adopted
stray       adopted not adopted
  not stray    0.44        0.38
  stray        0.56        0.62
      

      Which of the following can be correctly inferred from the table?

      
        There is a 56% chance that an animal in the shelter was both taken in as a stray and eventually adopted.
        Among animals that were not adopted, 62% were taken in as strays.
        Roughly 82% of all animals in the shelter were not taken in as strays.
      

      
         1 only
         2 only
         3 only
         1 and 2 only
         1 and 3 only
         2 and 3 only
         All (1, 2, and 3)
         None

title: Foundations of Data Science
text:
Welcome!! 🐈
Dear reader,
This online textbook serves as your first engagement with the concepts and applications of data science. It also introduces you to R and RStudio, which will be the primary computing tool to assist you in making data-driven inferences in practice.
There is little-to-no expectation of you to be excellent navigators of one webpage and one desktop application. So it is highly recommended that as you immerse through this textbook, you read it while copying and executing the code blocks presented in each chapter. Making that a habit for whichever course this reference gets used boosts the chances of the pretension of learning to escape your soul. If you find yourself less inclined to follow that recommendation, then you can just probably go back home and take over your father’s car dealership, or help your mother with grocery shopping.
At any moment you struggle with the coding aspect, reach out to a colleague or to your professor or TA. The sooner you address the hurdle, the better. Your basic recipe for success includes allocating enough time for this and not devote way too much to whatever foolish activities your chapter mates are engaging in.
Take it easy! 😁
\(\small\textit{Dr. H}\)

title: 1  Probability
text:
Events, Rules, and Sources of Probability
A probability quantifies how likely an event will happen. The probability of some event is a number between 0 and 1, inclusive. i.e.
\[0\leq P(\textrm{event})\leq 1\]
In this chapter, you will get acquainted with the basics of probability with emphasis on learning how to estimate them using data.
There are three fundamental ways of describing probability in terms of events: simple, joint, and conditional.

title: 1  Probability
text:
1.0.1 Careful Counting
A simple probability is the chance that one event happens. The most common example is the probabilities we can compute from flipping a coin. Understanding the concept of sample space is crucial here, as it determines all possible outcomes of an experiment. In a coin flip, the sample space is {H, T}. Assuming it is a fair coin, then the probability of landing tails is 0.5 because there is one outcome of interest out of two possible outcomes.
This is an application of the counting principle. Assuming all outcomes are equally likely to happen, then
\[\textrm{probability of an event happening}=\dfrac{\#\;\textrm{of outcomes of interest}}{\textrm{total }\#\;\textrm{of outcomes}}.\]
How about a slightly more interesting (and not as boring as coin flipping or dice tossing) scenario? What are the beverage and food preferences of a customer dining at a cafe?
